title: The Nanjing Incident: An Examination of the Civilian Population
strengths: Focused city-level denominator study using committee documents and Japanese registration evidence; explicitly rejects inflated city-population figures.
limitations: Best for city proper rather than the wider 'Nanking and vicinity' problem; not a mortality source; population changes after the fall still require interpolation for longer windows.
notes: Core denominator source for Nanjing city episodes.

title: Defending Nanking: An Examination of the Capital Garrison Forces
geographic_scope: Nanjing city and immediate battle approaches
strengths: Best accessible reconstruction of Chinese garrison size and battle timing; also reports Japanese force size and Japanese losses.
limitations: Does not solve the post-collapse fate of the Chinese defenders; not designed as a civilian death study; battle-period geography still requires researcher judgment.
notes: Core military denominator source for Nanjing city episodes.

title: War Damage in the Nanking Area: December 1937 to March 1938
pages_or_sections: City Survey pp. 4 and 8; Table 1; Table 25
geographic_scope: Nanjing city plus broader Nanking area rural hsien
strengths: Near-contemporaneous field survey with explicit city population and a narrow city civilian-violence estimate; also provides broader rural context.
limitations: City civilian death estimate excludes unarmed/disarmed soldiers; military deaths remain unresolved; rural tables cover a broader and less tidy theater than the city episodes used here.
notes: Key city-civilian source, but broader rural data should not be folded mechanically into city episodes.

title: Judgment, International Military Tribunal for the Far East, Volume II, Part B, Chapter VIII
author_or_org: International Military Tribunal for the Far East
strengths: Explicitly distinguishes several victim categories and gives side attribution to Japanese forces; especially valuable for the initial occupation window and POW killings.
limitations: Legal-historical synthesis rather than an operational incident database; overlap between categories is unresolved; 'vicinity' is broader than a city-core polygon.
notes: Use selectively. The >200,000 six-week total is retained as legal context, not as a core DR numerator.

title: Shanghai and the Experience of War: The Refugee Problem
pages_or_sections: text section 'Chinese refugees: an essay in quantification'
geographic_scope: Shanghai city, settlements, camps, and Jacquinot Safety Zone
strengths: Good city-level displacement and refugee-concentration evidence showing why a static Shanghai civilian denominator is hard to defend.
limitations: Does not provide a clean paired military denominator and civilian direct-death numerator for the full Battle of Shanghai.
notes: Used to justify non-estimability / further splitting for Shanghai.

title: August 1937: War and the Death en masse of Civilians
geographic_scope: Shanghai city center and foreign settlements
strengths: Strong incident-level detail for the 14 August bombing and immediate civilian losses.
limitations: This incident-scale source does not solve the wider battle's rolling population exposure or military denominator problem.
notes: Useful for a Shanghai micro-episode, but not enough by itself for the whole urban campaign.

title: Moving the Enemy: Operational Art in the Chinese PLA’s Huai Hai Campaign
geographic_scope: Xuzhou-Bengbu / Grand Canal / Long-Hai railroad theater
strengths: Excellent campaign geography, dates, and civilian labor-mobilization context for Huaihai.
limitations: Not a civilian death study and therefore insufficient on its own for a DR numerator.
notes: Supports why Huaihai needs a narrower sub-theater plus a separate civilian direct-death source stack before DR estimation.
